response:
# Complex Modal Dialog with Multi-Section Form Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver), keyboard-only users, low-vision users, touch users, cognitive-load-sensitive users > **Assistive technologies:** JAWS + Chrome, NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari, keyboard-only, touch (mobile screen readers) **Feature:** A reusable React modal dialog containing a variable-length, multi-section form with on-blur and on-submit validation, a focus trap, focus restoration to the trigger, Escape-to-close, and support for stacking multiple modals. **Risk Level:** High (modal + focus trap + focus restoration + form validation + stacking — four high-risk patterns combined) **Component Type:** Modal Dialog (WAI-ARIA APG Dialog (Modal) pattern) wrapping a Form --- ## Scope & Context **What is being built?** A reusable, accessible modal dialog component for a React e-commerce application that renders a multi-section form with mixed input types (text, select, checkbox, radio), validates on blur and on submit, traps keyboard focus, restores focus to the triggering control on close, and can be stacked (one modal opening another). **What user need does it address?** Users need to complete a structured data-entry task (e.g., checkout details, address book entry, payment method) inside a focused overlay that does not lose them in the background page, that tells screen reader users what context they are in, and that returns them exactly where they were when they close. **Who needs accessibility?** - **Screen reader users** — need the dialog announced as a dialog with its name, need the background made inert so they cannot wander into it, need form fields labeled and errors associated, need validation results announced. - **Keyboard-only users** — need every control reachable by Tab, need focus trapped so Tab never escapes to the hidden background, need Escape to close, need focus returned to the trigger. - **Low-vision users** — need a visible, high-contrast focus indicator on every focusable element, need the dialog usable at 200% zoom and 400% reflow. - **Touch users (including mobile screen reader users)** — need 44×44px targets and a usable close affordance; need the dialog to behave under VoiceOver rotor navigation. - **Cognitive-load-sensitive users** — need clear section grouping, plain-language errors with suggested fixes, and a predictable close/cancel model. **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA (default). **Assistive technologies to support:** JAWS (latest, with Chrome), NVDA (latest, with Firefox), VoiceOver (macOS Safari + iOS Safari), keyboard-only, screen magnifier, high-contrast / forced-colors mode, `prefers-reduced-motion`. **Risk level:** **High.** This is the canonical hard case. Focus trap bugs, missing focus restoration, and unassociated form errors are the three most common modal accessibility defects shipped to production, and stacking multiplies all three. The form-validation layer adds live-region and error-association risk on top of the dialog risk. **Existing code this modifies/extends:** None. New component. No `file:line` references to existing implementation — this is greenfield, so the plan specifies the target structure rather than diffing current state. **Constraints:** - Form size is variable (10+ fields) — initial-focus and scroll strategy must not assume a fixed layout; the dialog body must scroll independently while the trap still holds. - Multiple modals can be stacked — only the topmost modal may be interactive; every modal below it AND the page root must be inert; closing the top modal must restore focus to whatever opened it (which may be a control inside the modal beneath, not the page). - Touch support needed — target sizing and a visible, reachable close control are mandatory; the trap must not depend on pointer behavior. - React — hidden-content strategy should prefer **conditional rendering / portal mounting** over `display:none` toggling; focus moves must survive React re-render timing (use refs + a deferred focus apply). **Negative space (what this plan does NOT cover):** It does not specify the visual design system tokens (colors/spacing are referenced as contract requirements, not values), does not cover the server-side validation API, does not cover non-modal dialogs (`aria-modal` is assumed true throughout), and does not cover full-page route-level focus on navigation (separate concern). A *non-modal* variant, a *drawer*, or an *alertdialog* confirmation would each be a separate pattern decision; this plan is scoped to a single reusable modal dialog and the stacking of instances of that same component. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan ### Landmark and document relationship The modal is rendered into a **React portal appended to `document.body`**, OUTSIDE the main page DOM subtree, so that: 1. The `aria-modal="true"` + inerting of siblings reliably removes the background from the accessibility tree. 2. The dialog is not nested inside a landmark (`
`, `
`) that would give it a misleading landmark context. When the modal is open, the page background container (the app root, e.g. `#root`) and any modals beneath the top one receive **`inert`** (which also implies `aria-hidden` semantics and removes them from tab order). `inert` is the correct primitive here because `aria-hidden="true"` alone does NOT remove elements from the tab order — keyboard users could still Tab into the hidden background. WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard, WCAG 4.1.2 Name Role Value. ### Heading hierarchy - The dialog has a single visible title rendered as a heading. Because the dialog content is in a portal at the document level and represents a self-contained context, the title is an `

` (the page `

` remains the page title). The dialog title is the dialog's accessible name via `aria-labelledby`. - Form sections each get a heading that is ALSO the `` of the section's `
` (the legend is the accessible group name; a redundant separate heading is avoided to prevent double announcement). If a richer visual heading is required, the `` may contain a styled element, but there is exactly one programmatic group name per section. - No heading levels are skipped: dialog title `

`, section legends function as the next grouping level. WCAG 1.3.1, WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels. ### Form structure - The whole form is a single `
` element with `novalidate` (so the browser's native bubble validation does not compete with the planned accessible validation) — validation is owned by the component. - Each logical section is a `
` with a `` (e.g., "Shipping address", "Payment", "Preferences"). `
`/`` is required for radio groups and is the correct grouping for related inputs. WCAG 1.3.1. - **Radio groups** are themselves a `
`+`` (the question is the legend; each radio has its own `

_benchmark.method: plan 006 Phase D Claude lane: Agent(subagent_type=a11y-planner, model=opus), one background subagent per fixture; verbatim PLANNER_PROMPT_PREFIX task; agent wrote plan via Write tool; orchestrator wrapped to response JSON
